rulings.Case Title: FLICK, INC. VS. MING CHAO LIU
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Flick Inc.'S Motion To Compel On Dec 16 2024 Discovery Hearing
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 1. RESPONDENT MING LIU's Motion To Set Aside Order Granting Flick Inc.'s Motion To Compel On December 16, 2024. Continued to March 26, 2025, and transferred to department 302 at 9:00 a.m. to be heard by Judge Quinn, who ruled on the underlying motion to compel discovery on 12/16/24. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL VS. SANTOS & URRUTIA ASSOCIATES, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Deposition For Alex Santos
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 8. PLAINTIFFS CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, AND THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion To Compel Deposition For Alex Santos. Plaintiff City & County of San Francisco's unopposed motion to compel third-party witness Alex Santos to comply with its subpoena for deposition testimony and documents is granted. The Rosenblit declaration demonstrates that the City served the subpoena (Ex. G) and the witness did not appear (Ex. H). The City's counsel attempted to contact Santos's attorney when Santos did not appear. (Rosenblit Dec. para. 13.) The prerequisites for relief under CCP 2025.450 are satisfied. Accordingly, the court orders Santos to appear for deposition and produce the documents specified in the subpoena within 10 court days of entry of this order, or later if the parties mutually agree. The parties shall meet and confer about the date of deposition within five court days of entry of this order. If Santos through counsel does not meet and confer within that time, the City may reset the deposition pursuant to this order without clearing the date with Santos. The City also seeks sanctions. CCP 2025.450(g)(1) states, "If a motion under subdivision (a) is granted, the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) in favor of the party who noticed the deposition and against the deponent or the party with whom the deponent is affiliated, unless the court finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court does not find substantial justification here and awards sanctions. The court finds the City's counsel's hourly rate reasonable but reduces the number of hours to reflect that no opposition was filed and thus no reply brief was required. Santos shall pay $5990.71 to the City within 10 court days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: JASON COOK VS. CF COLLECTIVE, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Second Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant Cf Collective, Llc To Plaintiffs Form Interrogatories Employment Law, Set One, And For Terminating, Issue, And/Or Monetary Sanctions; Memorandum Of Points An (Motions)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 9. 1 - PLAINTIFF JASON COOK's Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant Cf Collective, Llc To Plaintiff's Form Interrogatories Employment Law, Set One, And For Terminating And/Or Monetary Sanctions. Plaintiff Jason Cook moves to compel further responses from defendant CF Collective, LLC to certain of his employment law form interrogatories, set one. The court orders the motion off calendar because it is subject to a stay by virtue of all defendants' appeal from this court's order of September 26, 2024, ordering further responses to this discovery and imposing sanctions. (See CCP 916(a) ["Except as provided in Sections 917.1 to 917.9, inclusive, and in Section 116.810, the perfecting of an appeal stays proceedings in the trial court upon the judgment or order appealed from or upon the matters embraced therein or affected thereby, including enforcement of the judgment or order, but the trial court may proceed upon any other matter embraced in the action and not affected by the judgment or order."].) Although only the sanctions portion of the order is appealed, the meaning and effect of the court's September 26 order, which is what Cook seeks to enforce here, is a "matter[] embraced therein or affected" by whether defendants acted with substantial justification in objecting to the discovery that was covered by the September 26 order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: DILLON JOHNSTONE VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC., ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 10. PLAINTIFF DILLON JOHNSTONE's Motion For Protective Order. Plaintiff Dillon Johnstone seeks a protective order limiting the discovery that Uber Technologies, Inc.; Rasier LLC; and Rasier-CA, LLC may serve on him and relieving him of the obligation to respond to already-propounded discovery by Uber. Those three defendants oppose and seek sanctions. The court grants Johnstone's motion in part and denies it in part. Uber has propounded 228 special interrogatories, 138 requests for admission, and 145 requests for production of documents. This is a plainly excessive number in light of the claims and defenses in this case. The court finds Johnstone's meet and confer efforts sufficient in light of the parties' respective positions in those letters and in the motion papers; no continued efforts at informal resolution would have bridged this gap. The court grants the protective order in part, limiting Uber to no more than 40 special interrogatories, 40 requests for admission, and 40 requests for production absent further order of the court. The topics Uber has covered with its current discovery can easily be combined into this or a smaller number of requests that will yield no less information but be considerably less burdensome to answer. Johnstone is relieved of responding to the discovery attached at Exhibits A through C to the Calloway declaration. Johnstone asks the court to limit all defendants to the number it has set. The court does not grant relief against parties who have not yet propounded discovery but urges the parties to apply the guidance in this order to future discovery disputes. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JOHN CRESS VS. SPEAR INVESTMENT GROUP, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing And Motion To Compel
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 5 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). DEFENDANT MIGUEL YNCLINO's Motion To Compel. Defendant and cross-complainant Miguel Ynclino's motion to compel responses to written discovery and request for sanctions from plaintiff and cross-defendant John Cress is denied. Cress initially sued Ynclino. Later, Ynclino cross-claimed against Cress. The cross-complaint was stricken in its entirety by this court on November 20, 2024. Ynclino has appealed that order. Ynclino's motion acknowledges this court's November 20 order, and the automatic stay of proceedings related to the cross-complaint (which has been stricken and no longer provides a basis for discovery or any relief in this court). But he contends that his discovery is nonetheless relevant to Cress's claims against him, which are not stayed. The problem is that Ynclino has not carried his burden, as the moving party, showing his entitlement to relief. His assertions of relevance are extremely general and broad. He has not provided a separate statement of disputed discovery requests, as required by Rule 3.1345. This requirement is no mere technicality; it enables the court to assess for itself whether objections are proper. The court cannot assess whether Cress's objections are well taken or not based on what Ynclino has submitted. Compounding the issue, Ynclino has not provided the discovery itself or Cress's responses. The court has no basis on this record to find that Cress's objections are not well taken. Accordingly the court denies Ynclino's motion to compel. Cress seeks sanctions. Under the relevant statutes, a party who unsuccessfully makes a motion to compel further responses to discovery shall be subject to monetary sanctions unless the party acted with substantial justification or other circumstances make imposition of the sanction unjust. The court finds that Ynclino acted without substantial justification here; a party representing himself is held to the same procedural requirements as a represented party. Nonetheless the court finds some mitigating circumstances and awards sanctions of $1000 to Cress. Ynclino shall pay the sanctions award within 10 court days of entry of this order. One further note. Cress does not argue here that all discovery is stayed by virtue of Ynclino's appeal of the order striking his cross-complaint. But the stricken cross-complaint alleges that Cress breached implied terms of an investment contract among Spear Investment Group LLC, Cress, and Ynclino. Cress's complaint, in turn alleges fraud and negligent misrepresentation by Spear and Ynclino in the course of formation of that contract. CCP section 916(a) provides that "the perfecting of an appeal stays proceedings in the trial court upon the judgment or order appealed from or upon the matters embraced therein or affected thereby . . . but the trial court may proceed upon any other matter embraced in the action and not affected by the judgment or order." The court has considered whether it has jurisdiction to issue the above order. It finds that there is significant overlap between the "matters embraced" in the complaint and cross-complaint, which may well stay proceedings in this court going to the merits of the complaint. However, it finds that the discovery order above is "not affected by the judgment or order" appealed from because it is decided on procedural grounds. Going forward, the parties should address 916(a) and its effect on the court's ability to decide the relief they seek in future motions until the appeal is resolved. (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). =(301/CVA)

rulings.Case Title: HARTFORD FIRE INSURANCE COMPANY VS. CARLOS MCCLATCHY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Responses To Plaintiffs Form Rogs (Set One), Special Rogs (Set One) And Request For Production Of Documents (Set One); Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Request For Sanctions Against Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 11. 1 - PLAINTIFF HARTFORD FIRE INSURANCE COMPANY , INDIVIDUALLY AND AS SUBROGEE FOR ITS INSURED, PIETRO MANAGEMENT COMPANY's Motion To Compel Responses To Plaintiff's Form Rogs (Set One), Special Rogs (Set One) And Request For Production Of Documents (Set One); Request For Sanctions Against Defendant. Plaintiff Hartford Fire Insurance Company's motion to compel defendant Carlos McClatchy to respond to its Form Interrogatories, Set One; Special Interrogatories, Set One; and Requests for Production of Documents, Set One, is granted. Defendant failed to timely respond to this discovery and has thereby waived objections. (CCP 2030.290(a) & 2031.300(a).) Defendant is ordered to serve verified responses, without objections, accompanied by documents responsive to the requests for production within 15 court days of entry of this order. Plaintiff also seeks sanctions. CCP 2030.290(c) provides that "the court shall impose a monetary sanction . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion to compel a response to interrogatories . . . unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." CCP 2031.300(c) contains an analogous provision with respect to requests for production. The court does not find substantial justification here. The court reduces the sanctions amount because no opposition or reply briefs were filed. The court awards sanctions of $1480.75 to Plaintiff payable by Defendant. Defendant shall remit sanctions within 15 court days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: HARTFORD FIRE INSURANCE COMPANY VS. CARLOS MCCLATCHY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Establishing Admissions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 11. 2 - PLAINTIFF HARTFORD FIRE INSURANCE COMPANY, INDIVIDUALLY AND AS SUBROGEE FOR ITS INSURED, PIETRO MANAGEMENT COMPANY's Motion For Order Establishing Admissions. Plaintiff Hartford Insurance Company's motion to deem admitted the truth of any matters specified in its Requests for Admissions, Set One, is granted. Defendant Carlos McClatchy failed to timely respond to this discovery and has thereby waived objections. (CCP 2033.280(a).) The truth of any matters specified in the RFAs, Set One, is deemed admitted by Plaintiff. (CCP 2033.280(b), (c).) Plaintiff also seeks sanctions. CCP 2033.280(c) further provides: "It is mandatory that the court impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) on the party or attorney, or both, whose failure to serve a timely response to requests for admission necessitated this motion." The court awards sanctions but reduces the requested amount. Plaintiff's estimate was based on the assumption that the motion would be opposed, and it was not. The court awards $1480.75 in sanctions payable by Defendant to Plaintiff. Defendant shall remit sanctions within 15 court days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. VS. FENG HUANG
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT / Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion / Discovery calendar for Wednesday, March 12, 2025, Line 6 (Part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). PLAINTIFF WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT, Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Plaintiff Wells Fargo Bank N.A. seeks summary judgment against defendant Feng Huang. This case arises out of Wells Fargo's issuance of a credit card to Huang. Wells Fargo brings a cause of action for breach of contract and a cause of action for common count open book account money due. Huang has not opposed the motion. For the reasons stated herein, the court grants summary judgment to Wells Fargo. "A party may move for summary judgment in an action or proceeding if it is contended that the action has no merit or that there is no defense to the action or proceeding." (CCP 437c(a)(1).) "[T]he party moving for summary judgment bears an initial burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any triable issue of material fact; if he carries his burden of production, he causes a shift, and the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to make a prima facie showing of the existence of a triable issue of material fact." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850.) "[I]f a plaintiff who would bear the burden of proof by a preponderance of evidence at trial moves for summary judgment, he must present evidence that would require a reasonable trier of fact to find any underlying material fact more likely than not-otherwise, he would not be entitled to judgment as a matter of law . . . ." (Id. at p. 851.) Wells Fargo has satisfied its burden. On the common count open book account claim, its evidence shows that Huang opened a credit card account, accepted terms of service creating a contract with Wells Fargo, and accrued a balance. (Dennis Dec. paras. 6-9 & Exs. A & B.) Its evidence shows that Wells Fargo maintained a record of the account and periodically mailed statements to Huang (Dennis Dec. para. 10 & Ex. C), and that Huang owed a balance of $8,029.19 when he stopped payments on the account (Dennis Dec. para. 12). On the same evidence, Wells Fargo has satisfied its burden as to the breach of contract claim: the parties formed a contract whereby Wells Fargo issued credit to Huang and Huang agreed to repay it; Wells Fargo substantially performed its obligations under the contract; Huang breached the contract by failing to do something it required him to do (repay his balance), and Wells Fargo was harmed by his nonperformance. The court grants summary judgment to Wells Fargo. Wells Fargo shall submit a proposed judgment. (End of part 1 of 2 of the tentative ruling). =(301/CVA)
